text: Saw an early screening of KPOP DEMON HUNTERS and came out PUMPED. It is basically Sailor Moon meets Buffy the Vampire Slayer, but make it K-pop. Sony Animation is on-a-roll. You have funny, badass and messy young women who can sing, dance and also protect people from soul-consuming demons. Add on a very hot demon boy bands and some enemies to lovers trope...it's chef's kiss! The animation is gorgeous, the songs slap and in some cases an adrenaline rush. I'll be playing it on repeat on Spotify for sure.

This is AWESOME.
So vibrant and alive. Sony Animation’s action sequences always look killer and the way this incorporates music into the fights looks incredible, offering a really fun language to translate these fights through. Got a bit weepy at the end because it does such a good job at constructing its central characters’ friendships with one another. Lots of great ideas and character designs and stylistic choices that all come together to make something satisfying and really, really delightful to experience.

text:
Half of the songs sound like knockoff K/DA, and the other half has as much in common with Kpop as something by the Weeknd or Lady Gaga. Surely, the real demons all along are the production companies that have been sucking the lifeblood dry from these hollowed out idols. But of course, a film like Kpop Demon Hunter can only spin it all into yet another insecurity to self acceptance arc; the same story retold, recycled, and shoved into a tired three act structure as uninspired as its soundtrack.
"The couch can wait", but the exploitation can't.
4/10.

author: Unknown
text:
There was indeed K-Pop, and demons were hunted, that much is true. As a concrete work, this reminded me more than anything of Frozen, albeit with a lot more life pumped into its comedic elements and visual properties. As a rule I generally prefer musical films in which the numbers are more integrated into the story and the actions of the characters, rather than just a break from any sort of narrative tension with some tenuously connected lyrics. But for what it is, it's pleasant, engaging and very funny. You could certainly do a lot worse.

But the story itself is primarily focused on Rumi (Arden Cho) and how she navigates her career as a half-demon amidst the rise of a competitor boy group, the Saja Boys. author: Unknown
text:
With a title like KPop Demon Hunters, I expected something silly and low-effort, but Sony Animation and the filmmakers delivered a quality movie.
While it wraps things up a little too neatly, and the story is basically Blade Runner 2.0, the maturity at times is welcoming, the experimental animation is always a blast to watch, and the songs are surprisingly catchy. Call me crazy, but 'What It Sounds Like' sounds a tad familiar to Calvin Harris and Ellie Goulding's Outside, lol.

text:
Neste momento, "Golden" só é menos famoso que "Let it Go", e por uma margem muito pequena.
O que é totalmente merecido; Kim Eun-Jae arrasou na composição dessas músicas. O filme, além de ser visualmente belíssimo, é um excelente musical, e não há como negar.
English
At this point, "Golden" is only less famous than "Let it Go," and that's by a very small margin.
Which is totally deserved; Kim Eun-Jae nailed it composing those songs. The film, besides being visually beautiful, is an excellent musical, and there's no way to contradict that.

author: Unknown
text:
Stan Huntr/x. Believe the hype. This movie is a blast.
KPop Demon Hunters is vibrantly animated and brimming with punchy action sequences and infectious earworms (will “Golden” ever leave my head? Only time will tell). The story is refreshingly simple, which in and of itself is a feat given how unruly this mash-up concept could easily become. Some plot points are clunky and there are underdeveloped character arcs, but this never really feels like it’s to the detriment of the film. It’s certainly not the best animated movie as of late, but it’s probably the most fun one I’ve watched in recent memory.

author: Unknown
text:
The fact that about 5 grown adults (2 of which are older than me) heralded Kpop Demon Hunter as the best movie they've seen all year is just straight up wild to me.
It's a simple, animated tale of self-acceptance and self-expression stretched out to 96 minutes. To be frank, it's just straight-washed pride.
The songs are catchy but shallow, the acting leaves much to be desired, the animation is good but not great, the pacing is very rushed, and the humor never lands.
For all the praise this movie recieves, I can think of several examples that far exceed it.

text: The people who have loved the KPOP explosion for the last decade are finally making movies the difference is immediate. It's goes from treating it like a reference point, to being utterly and holistically imbued in an entire world of reference points, while having the spirit of the thing utterly ingrained. As a film, the first half is fun as heck. The second half gets a case of the "second act-sies" and you spend a lot of time waiting for them to actually re-engage the story they're telling, but they land the proverbial plane. Super fun. Two of the songs are really great. Really liked it.

author: Unknown
text:
A vibrant, anime-inspired romp where a K-pop girl group doubles as demon slayers, battling a demonic boy band to seal a magical barrier.
Catchy earworms like "Golden" and "Takedown" fuel high-energy fight scenes, while the fluid animation bursts with cool-toned flair and infectious joy.
At its heart, it's a heartfelt tale of identity, fandom, and sisterhood, though the rushed third act leaves some emotional beats underdeveloped.

Could have been just the music and I certainly wouldn't have complained. But then again, I'm not complaining now

Late to the party on this one, as I thought this was something that didn't seem up my alley, but I was wrong, because I loved every second of watching KPop Demon Hunters. It's both funny and visually appealing to watch, while also conveying the core messages of friendship and self-acceptance that are easy to understand for younger viewers and yet still impactful enough for older ones. Not to mention, a killer soundtrack. Definitely unlocked a new comfort film and a new fictional artist to listen to, Hunter/x feat. Skye Riley, when?

text: Bright, colourful, and poppy animation, banger songs, and some excellent comedic bits. You really don't need to be into k-pop to enjoy this, but there's a lot here that'll be extra funny/charming for fans, great balance that way. text:
Dont miss it for the world.
What a fun movie, maybe a little overhyped, but entertaining nonetheless.
The animation is stunning, the voice acting is so good, the writing is pretty clever, and that damn soundtrack is fire. Banger after banger. Give them their deserving Oscar already.
The story is nothing groundbreaking, the villain is very underwhelming, and the demons design wasn't that creative, but I get the hype.
Haven't seen many animated movies from this year, but I wouldn't be upset if this one gets the gold.

author: Unknown
text:
Now from someone who doesn’t know much K-Pop, and the furthest I can go with my K-Pop intelligence is fucking BTS, I really really loved this film. 
A truly triumphant piece in magic realism as KPop Demon Hunters provides an exquisitely original narrative, conjuring up some beautiful music and visuals that never feel lazy, artificial or mundane. Just a bit more of an easier landing regarding the lore and prologue and we would have a serious contender for best animated feature. 
But, honestly, I’m really surprised with how much of a good time I had with KPop Demon Hunters.

text:
2025 Catch Up 3/4.
This is exactly the kind of chaotic genre mashup I didn’t know I needed, a neon-drenched fever dream where glitter, group choreography, and demonic possession collide in gloriously over-the-top fashion. Imagine if Blade joined forces with The Exorcist at a Seoul music festival, and you’re halfway there.
The third act loses some steam as it tries to juggle too many plot threads, maybe if the movie was a bit longer it could have fleshed stuff out better, but it’s a wild ride nonetheless.

text:
FEARLESS AND UNDEFINED
Singing along with a bunch of families and their kids in the cinema was definitely an experience. And the most wholesome thing is witnessing some of the kids jumping along to the last few songs at the front of the theatre.
When it reached What it Sounds Like, I was almost ready to well up singing along. The lyrics are incredibly affirming and emotionally resonant. And it's just such a beautiful song that captures the whole arc of the movie. The harmonies, the thumping beat, the crowd chanting as Rumi starts to level up. It was just pure fun.

I put this off for way too long. The title didn’t grab me, and I wrongly assumed it was Korean-language. But then, the hype got too loud to ignore. Turns out, I missed out. 
The animation is slick, the action slaps, and the world-building is way better than it has any right to be. I’m not as high on the songs as most people, and the switch between voice actors and singing voices is distracting at times.
Still. The Huntrix rules! I love the trio. Worth the watch.
Oscars’ Best Animated Feature incoming. Surely.

author: Unknown
text:
I love being pleasantly surprised by something I initially figured wouldn't be my thing.
The majority of media released nowadays is connected to an already well-known franchise, so it's nice to still get original movies that will almost certainly start their own franchise. KPop Demon Hunters has spectacular animation that is filled with stunning visuals and personality, and there is no other animated film that looks or moves like it. The voice acting is excellent, the story is compelling, and the music is EPIC. I had so much fun with this, and I look forward to seeing this become its own hit franchise.

author: Unknown
text:
After the passing of his wife, C. S. Lewis famously wrote “feelings, feelings, feelings. Let me try thinking instead”. 
Lewis goes on to write that even the strongest, most intimate feelings can be deceitful, an echo of the Scriptures he so enjoyed.
Even though Hollywood has adapted Lewis’s works to the big screen before, the industry went full steam ahead in the opposite direction: my feelings dictate your truth. 
Which is why it’s so refreshing to see an animated work that dares to suggest we’re no better than demons when we let emotion alone dictate the course of our lives.

Really not my thing, aesthetically (music, visuals, pacing, sense of humor, etc.), but thematically, it's surprisingly, even shockingly wise about how shame is perhaps the ultimate weapon spiritual evil wields to create "patterns" of thought and behavior that divide and isolate parts of God's good creation from the whole. Pretty much Curt Thompson's The Soul of Shame in movie form; reminded me of not one but two conversations I've had with priests in the last month. I'm glad the little brother got me to watch it with him.
Weird how this came out the same year as Sinners.

text: I expected not to like this. This is really not my thing, and yet as I kept watching it grew on me. I hate KPop and yet the songs are a lot catchier than I want to admit. The animation is full of those quirky stylings and weird frame rates that Spider-Verse has inflicted on the genre now, but I can look past most of that. The liar revealed story is tedious, but the character relationships and arcs are actually pretty decently done. It’s also just a fun movie. A bit overrated, but I liked more than I might like to admit.

Any characters outside of the three leading ladies are a bit thin and superficial, but that does let the personalities of the ladies shine through.

text: Rewatched this with the discord community! Thankfully most of them hadn't seen it before. Sony has really consistently been setting the bar when it comes to animation experimentation, and honestly, this film deserves all the praise it gets imo. It manages to play with the classic 'magically inclined girls with secret identity" trope and still make it feel incredibly fresh and like something you've never quite seen before. I don't even like K pop, but I love this film, so that should say a lot. Really hope we get a sequel eventually.

text:
The little Netflix movie that could.
KPOP was part of a 2021 post-pandemic deal between Sony and Netlfix where Sony would develop and produce, and Netflix would cover the full budgets plus a 25%-of-budget premium in exchange for all rights worldwide upon release. KPOP had a $100M budget - small by mainstream animation standards where most Pixar features are $250M. Sony was paid $100M + $25M by Netflix.
To the streaming service's credit, they took KPOP out to toy companies,… more

text: Very impressed by this super-confident Western take on the kind of content kids over here have loved since the late ‘90s. (Seriously, why did it take so long?) The initial lack of a theatrical release indicates some anxiety over the material, but the last few months have proven that audiences are way into any alternative from the Disney/Pixar house style. Maybe I’d like a few more jokes here and there, but the fact that we have a rare animated film not driven by daddy issues should be celebrated.

author: Unknown
text: Slick visuals & empty cliches. A good match between style and subject, since the hypercapitalist pop world evolved to present the lack of any veil of authenticity as a feature instead of a bug and everything here from the monsters' design to the mandatory depiction of a panic attack come off as supremely derivative. Yeah, a couple of the songs are catchy, but I don't think I'm being too much of a grouch for wanting a bit more out of big-budget animation.

text:
The visuals are consistently stunning bright, bold and full of energy. The music is genuinely great, they hit the kpop vibe and the action intense vibe when needed!
What stands out, though, is the film’s exploration of shame. Beneath the flashy performances and high stakes demon battles, there’s a surprisingly reflective look at how shame shapes people, the way it isolates, lingers and can even become something monstrous if un dealt with.

I would say "Good for this movie for winning an Oscar", but after their comment on how some of the voters don't bother to watch the animated movies, it should really be apparent that the Oscar win doesn't mean shit.
I'm not saying this movie is bad, far from it, but we should start to realize that just because a movie won an Oscar, it doesn't necessarily mean it holds superiority over another. And considering how badly the Academy has gotten recently, we need to stop taking them seriously.
This can also be said for most award shows, but my point still stands.

author: Unknown
text:
KPop Demon Hunters reinvents the idea of overcoming shame, and the idea of accepting one's self. I say this with the context of the pressing winning speech by one of the women awarded an Oscar last night. KPop Demon Hunters achieved a beautiful mixture of cultural display, and global appreciation -- allowing Korean culture to thrive in all it's modern artistic beauty and making it wildly accessible for the rest of the world.
There are no other notes. Fantastic, colorful display of fighting against one's own 'demons' of shame and internal-ridicule, and learning to love who you are, and what you represent.
